text: Thank you for inviting me for this very important meeting, ACRLS, and today my presentation is about robotic esophagectomy. And robotic esophagectomy is nowadays very commonly performed in our country, and the application of this procedure is spreading very fast around the world. And I am briefly showing some of the concept of robotic esophagectomy and show some video clip. And lastly, I show data or outcome of our hospital.

Currently, we have one randomized control trial comparing robotic esophagectomy to open esophagectomy. According to the paper published in two thousand nineteen, the short term outcomes of robotic esophagectomy were better than the open esophagectomy. So it can be considered that robotic esophagectomy may be one of the good options as a minimally invasive esophagectomy.

However, there are some heterogeneity of terminology and some of the confusion in the terminology of robotic surgery. And most of the studies published about the robotic surgery. Most of the surgery was not done totally robotic technique. Usually robotic dissection was done in the chest and anastomosis was done by thoracoscopic technique and furthermore in the abdominal procedure usually laparoscopic technique was used mostly. So most of the papers were not true robotic surgery. It was just a hybrid procedure. robotic and thoracoscopic and laparoscopic surgery.

In our hospital, we started through robotic surgery, doing the whole procedure using robot and thoracic and abdominal from the year of the year of the year of the year, so today's video clip is our current technique we are using when we do the routine esophagectomy.

The first video clip that I show is the abdominal procedure. The abdominal procedure in their esophagectomy is composed of very complex procedures. For example, abdominal lymphoma dissection and omental division and graft formation and pyloromytomy and coca procedure, jejunal stomy, very, very complex and long-time procedure. However, because of the shortage of time, I'll show you just a lymphoma dissection and graft formation in this video clip.
The surgeon identifies the pancreas and grasps the perigastric tissue using robotic instruments during the esophagectomy procedure.

The surgeon removes peripancreatic tissue, performing dissection around the common hepatic artery. The visual field includes the portal hepatis, with no visible metastasis from the chest to the abdomen, indicating early-stage esophageal cancer.

The surgeon performs a moderate dissection around the common hepatic artery, revealing lymph nodes in the area. The lymph nodes are visible and may be labeled as either common hepatic or celiac lymph nodes.
The surgeon identifies lymph nodes around the hepatic artery, labeled as number eighteen according to the AJCC staging system.
The surgeon identifies the left gastric artery and creates a space around it. Dissection is performed along the artery's long axis, and the artery is encircled using webbing.
The surgeon applies double clips to secure the left gastric artery, ensuring safety by using additional clips for reinforcement.
Harmony? Okay. Okay, good.

After completing the dissection along the celiac axis, the surgeon has removed the tissue surrounding the common hepatic artery, splenic artery, and celiac axis. The surgeon then uses a green SureForm stapler to create a long graft with a large circumference, preparing for the first staple fire.

The surgeon holds up the omentum and pulls the lymphatic tissue upwards, positioning the stapler underneath the omental tissue to remove the lymph nodes around the stomach. The stomach is pulled to create a longer graft length.

The second video clip is about the thoracic procedure. Also, thoracic procedure is also a complex procedure. In terms of lymphoma dissection, it requires dissection from the neck to the diaphragm surface. Bilateral recurrent transgenic nerve dissection and subcarinal and paroesophageal lymphoma dissection is also a very complex procedure. However, in this video clip, I just showed right recurrent transgenic nerve dissection, which is a very representative procedure in thoracic lymphoma dissection. and gastric pull-up procedure, which will be necessary for the preparation of the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs right recurrent laryngeal nerve dissection as part of the esophagectomy, with the vagus nerve visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon exposes the vagus nerve and opens the mediastinal pleura along the subclavian artery. Using the third arm, the mediastinal pleura is held anteriorly, widening the surgical field. Gentle dissection is performed along the vagus nerve, revealing branches of nerves and vessels, including the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon focuses on the area between the recurrent laryngeal nerve and the esophagus, where cancer metastasis is commonly found. The goal is to remove all tissue in this region, which requires careful dissection due to the proximity of the nerve.

The surgeon identifies the recurrent laryngeal nerve to prevent nerve injury, then removes surrounding tissues. Visible lymph nodes in the right upper thoracic area are highlighted, including the top pet lymph nodes, numbered as 2R in the AJCC system and known as 6R in the Japanese system, which are common sites of metastasis.

The surgeon continues to follow the recurrent laryngeal nerve, identifying and dividing vascular branches in the neck area. The inferior thyroid artery is visible crossing the surgical field. The thyroid gland is exposed as the dissection progresses deeper.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to remove tissue between the esophagus and recurrent laryngeal nerve, extending from the chest to the neck. The dissection includes cervical paraesophageal lymph nodes, which are visible and distinct from thoracic lymph nodes.
If you are going to do this using a sarcoscope, this may be a very, very challenging procedure. But using the rubber is easier. And clean surgical fields can be obtained. Yes.
The surgeon performs a precise dissection at a high level, revealing a lymph node identified as one of the 6R nodes according to the Japanese system.

The final view shows the esophagus completely separated, with all lymph nodes removed along the left and right recurrent laryngeal nerves. The trachea and major airways remain undamaged. The left pleura is intact, and the diaphragmatic lymph nodes have been fully excised. The thoracic duct is visible and undamaged. The surgeon prepares to pull up the stomach.

The surgeon visually assesses the suture site between the resected esophagus and the stomach graft conduit, adjusting the position of the graft to ensure proper tension and alignment without resistance.

Until now, currently we perform more than three hundred cases of robotic esophagectomy. However, total robotic esophagectomy was done around two hundred twenty patients. In those two hundred twenty patients, the conversion rate was zero point nine percent and ninety day mortality rate was zero point nine percent. considering the outcomes of previous reported papers, the conversion rate and the ninety-day mortality rate is very, very low.

And if you look at the long-term survival, the five-year survival of stage one was eighty-three percent, and stage two was fifty-eight percent, stage three was sixty-nine percent, and stage four was thirty-three percent. And the AJCC eighth staging system changes the supraclavicular lymph node metastasis from stage three to stage four. So they were the poor prognosis group in stage three in the seventh staging system. So because of the change of the classification, the survival of the stage three improved, and also survival of stage four also improved. Though there are some of the controversy in staging system, I think the overall survival of our totally robotic esophagectomy is quite favorable considering comparing all the previous reported papers.
